---
title: "Chapter 8: Descriptive Statistics for Consultants"
subtitle: "R for Management Consultants | BK Advisors"
author: "BK Advisors"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    logo: bka-logo.png
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    background-transition: slide
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-color: "#005CB9"
    hash-type: number
    center: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: false
  warning: false
---

## Learning Objectives

After this chapter, you will be able to:

::: {.incremental}
- Calculate **means, medians, and proportions** with confidence intervals
- Build **summary tables** suitable for stakeholder reports
- Compute **correlation** between two variables and interpret the result
- Run a **simple linear regression** with `lm()` and interpret the output
- Understand the difference between **statistical significance** and **practical significance**
- Connect descriptive statistics to the **causal reasoning** taught in inference-and-intervention
:::


## Chapter Overview

<div class="step-flow">
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #E24A3F;">Central<br>Tendency</div>
<div class="step-arrow">&rarr;</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #005CB9;">Proportions</div>
<div class="step-arrow">&rarr;</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #83BD00;">Confidence<br>Intervals</div>
<div class="step-arrow">&rarr;</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #FA7650;">Correlation</div>
<div class="step-arrow">&rarr;</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #3E9B6E;">Regression</div>
</div>


# Measures of Central Tendency {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Summarizing your data in one number</span>


## Mean vs. Median

```{r}
#| label: central-tendency
#| echo: true

nmr_values <- c(12.5, 18.3, 22.1, 25.0, 27.5, 31.7, 35.2, 42.1, 45.3, 68.0)

mean(nmr_values)    # 32.8 — pulled up by the 68.0 outlier
median(nmr_values)  # 29.6 — resistant to outliers

# When to use which?
# Mean:   symmetric distributions, no extreme outliers
# Median: skewed distributions, outliers present
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
NMR data across facilities is typically right-skewed (a few very high values). Report the median alongside the mean, and always note outliers.
:::


## Spread: Standard Deviation and IQR

```{r}
#| label: spread
#| echo: true

sd(nmr_values)   # standard deviation: 16.2
IQR(nmr_values)  # interquartile range: 17.0

# Quantiles
quantile(nmr_values, probs = c(0.25, 0.50, 0.75))
#  25%   50%   75%
# 23.2  29.6  37.0

# Range
range(nmr_values)  # 12.5 68.0
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**Standard deviation** assumes a roughly symmetric distribution. When data is skewed --- as MNH facility data often is --- the **IQR** (distance between the 25th and 75th percentiles) gives a more robust measure of spread.
:::


## Summary Statistics by Group

```{r}
#| label: group-stats
#| echo: true

library(dplyr)

facility_data |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_nmr = round(mean(nmr, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    median_nmr = round(median(nmr, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    sd_nmr = round(sd(nmr, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    min_nmr = min(nmr, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_nmr = max(nmr, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
**This is the table your stakeholders want.** A well-formatted group summary --- with n, mean, median, SD, and range --- answers most descriptive questions a ministry or donor will ask about program performance across countries.
:::


# Proportions & Cross-Tabulations {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">How many? What fraction?</span>


## Calculating Proportions

```{r}
#| label: proportions
#| echo: true

# Proportion of facilities with CPAP
mean(facility_data$has_cpap, na.rm = TRUE)  # e.g., 0.42 = 42%

# Proportion by country
facility_data |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    pct_cpap = round(mean(has_cpap, na.rm = TRUE) * 100, 1),
    pct_trained = round(mean(training_completed, na.rm = TRUE) * 100, 1)
  )
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**`mean()` on a logical or 0/1 column gives the proportion.** This is a handy shortcut: `TRUE` counts as 1, `FALSE` as 0, so the mean is the fraction of `TRUE` values. Always report the sample size `n` alongside proportions.
:::


## Cross-Tabulations

```{r}
#| label: cross-tabs
#| echo: true

# Two-way frequency table
table(facility_data$country, facility_data$facility_type)

# Proportions (row percentages)
prop.table(table(facility_data$country, facility_data$facility_type),
           margin = 1) |>
  round(2)

# Tidyverse alternative
facility_data |>
  count(country, facility_type) |>
  group_by(country) |>
  mutate(pct = round(n / sum(n) * 100, 1))
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .orange}
**Cross-tabs reveal composition differences.** If one country's facilities are 80% health posts while another is 50% hospitals, comparing raw NMR averages between countries is misleading --- facility type confounds the comparison.
:::


# Confidence Intervals {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">How certain are we?</span>


## What Is a Confidence Interval?

::: {.definition-box}
A **95% confidence interval** means: if we repeated this data collection many times, 95% of the intervals would contain the true population value. It quantifies uncertainty.
:::

. . .

```{r}
#| label: ci-basic
#| echo: true

# 95% CI for mean NMR
t.test(facility_data$nmr)

# Extract just the confidence interval
t.test(facility_data$nmr)$conf.int
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**Always report confidence intervals alongside point estimates.** Telling a ministry "the mean NMR is 28.5" is less useful than "the mean NMR is 28.5 (95% CI: 24.1 -- 32.9)." The interval communicates how much you should trust the number.
:::


## Confidence Intervals by Group

```{r}
#| label: ci-group
#| echo: true

# CI for mean NMR by country
facility_data |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_nmr = mean(nmr, na.rm = TRUE),
    se = sd(nmr, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n()),
    ci_lower = mean_nmr - 1.96 * se,
    ci_upper = mean_nmr + 1.96 * se
  )
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**The standard error (SE) shrinks as your sample grows.** SE = SD / sqrt(n). More facilities in the sample means a narrower confidence interval and more precise estimates. This is why sample size matters for program evaluation.
:::


## Visualizing Confidence Intervals

```{r}
#| label: ci-plot
#| echo: true

library(ggplot2)

country_ci <- facility_data |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(
    mean_nmr = mean(nmr, na.rm = TRUE),
    se = sd(nmr, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n()),
    ci_lower = mean_nmr - 1.96 * se,
    ci_upper = mean_nmr + 1.96 * se
  )

ggplot(country_ci, aes(x = reorder(country, mean_nmr), y = mean_nmr)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#005CB9") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = ci_lower, ymax = ci_upper),
                width = 0.2, color = "#005CB9") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Mean NMR by Country (with 95% CI)",
       x = NULL, y = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
**Overlapping confidence intervals** suggest the difference between groups may not be statistically significant. **Non-overlapping intervals** strongly suggest a real difference.
:::


# Correlation {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">How strongly are two variables related?</span>


## Pearson Correlation

```{r}
#| label: correlation
#| echo: true

# Correlation between delivery volume and NMR
cor(facility_data$delivery_volume, facility_data$nmr,
    use = "complete.obs")

# Correlation test with p-value and CI
cor.test(facility_data$delivery_volume, facility_data$nmr)

# Correlation matrix for multiple variables
facility_data |>
  select(delivery_volume, nmr, mmr, anc_coverage) |>
  cor(use = "complete.obs") |>
  round(2)
```


## Interpreting Correlation

| r Value | Strength | Example |
|---------|----------|---------|
| 0.0 -- 0.3 | Weak | ANC visits and facility cleanliness score |
| 0.3 -- 0.6 | Moderate | Staffing level and delivery volume |
| 0.6 -- 1.0 | Strong | Equipment availability and facility budget |

. . .

::: {.callout-box .red}
**Correlation measures linear association. It does NOT imply causation.** Two variables can be correlated because a third variable drives both (confounding). The inference-and-intervention course covers this in depth.
:::


# Simple Linear Regression {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Modeling relationships</span>


## lm() --- Fitting a Line

```{r}
#| label: lm-basic
#| echo: true

# Does delivery volume predict NMR?
model <- lm(nmr ~ delivery_volume, data = facility_data)
summary(model)
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**`lm(y ~ x, data)` fits a linear model.** The formula `nmr ~ delivery_volume` asks: "How does NMR change, on average, for each unit increase in delivery volume?" The output gives you the intercept, slope, and measures of fit.
:::


## Reading the Output

| Section | What It Tells You |
|---------|-------------------|
| **Coefficients: Estimate** | The slope and intercept. "For each additional delivery, NMR changes by ___." |
| **Std. Error** | Uncertainty around the estimate |
| **t value / Pr(>\|t\|)** | Statistical significance. p < 0.05 typically considered "significant." |
| **R-squared** | Proportion of variance explained. 0.15 means 15% of NMR variation is explained by delivery volume. |
| **F-statistic** | Overall model significance |

. . .

::: {.callout-box .orange}
**A statistically significant result (small p-value) is not the same as a practically important result.** A coefficient of 0.01 might be significant with enough data, but adding one delivery reduces NMR by only 0.01 --- trivial in practice.
:::


## Multiple Regression

```{r}
#| label: lm-multiple
#| echo: true

# Multiple predictors
model2 <- lm(nmr ~ delivery_volume + has_cpap + staffing_level + anc_coverage,
             data = facility_data)
summary(model2)

# Compare models
AIC(model, model2)  # lower AIC = better fit
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**Adding predictors can improve model fit, but more is not always better.** Each additional variable should have a substantive reason for inclusion. AIC helps you compare models: a lower AIC indicates a better balance between fit and complexity.
:::


## Visualizing the Regression

```{r}
#| label: lm-plot
#| echo: true

ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = delivery_volume, y = nmr)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "#005CB9") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#E24A3F", fill = "#E24A3F", alpha = 0.2) +
  labs(title = "NMR vs. Delivery Volume",
       subtitle = paste("R² =", round(summary(model)$r.squared, 3)),
       x = "Monthly Delivery Volume",
       y = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
**The shaded band is the confidence interval around the regression line.** A wider band means more uncertainty about the relationship. Points far from the line are poorly predicted by the model --- worth investigating individually.
:::


## Statistics to Causation: The Bridge

::: {.callout-box}
Everything in this chapter describes **associations** in data. To move from association to causation --- to answer "does X *cause* Y?" --- you need the causal modeling toolkit from the Inference and Intervention course. Descriptive statistics are the foundation; causal models are the structure you build on top.
:::

. . .

::: {.columns-equal}
::: {.col}
**What this chapter covers**

- Mean, median, SD, IQR
- Proportions and cross-tabs
- Confidence intervals
- Correlation and regression
- Association and prediction
:::

::: {.col}
**What causal models add**

- Confounders and colliders
- DAGs (directed acyclic graphs)
- Do-calculus and intervention
- Counterfactual reasoning
- Causal effect estimation
:::
:::


## Key Takeaways

::: {.key-takeaway}
**Always report uncertainty:** means with confidence intervals, proportions with sample sizes.

**Correlation measures association, not causation.** A strong correlation could be driven by a confounder.

**`lm()` fits a linear model.** Read R-squared for explanatory power and coefficients for effect sizes --- but remember: regression alone cannot establish causation.
:::


## Looking Ahead

::: {.callout-box}
**Next: Chapter 9 --- Reproducible Reports with Quarto**

- Combining narrative, code, and charts in one document
- Rendering to HTML, PDF, and DOCX for different audiences
- Parameterized reports: one template, five country briefs
:::


##  {background-color="white"}

<br><br><br>

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
![](bka-logo.png){width="250px"}

<br>

[R for Management Consultants | Chapter 8: Descriptive Statistics for Consultants]{style="color: #005CB9; font-size: 0.6em;"}

[BK Advisors]{style="color: #666; font-size: 0.5em;"}
:::
